hints.accusative: Accusative plural would be 'Litteras' — 'Litterae' with -ae is nominative plural
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would also be 'Litterae,' but here it is nominative plural as the subject
hints.vocative: Vocative plural would also be 'Litterae,' but letters are not being addressed

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'litterae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'litterae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'litterae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'signatae erant' translates to?
steps.target: to seal / to mark with a seal
choices: to seal / to mark with a seal

hints.to write: 'To write' is 'scribere' — 'signatae erant' comes from 'signare' (to seal/stamp)
hints.to sign: While related in English, 'signare' in Latin primarily means to seal or stamp with a signet
hints.to designate: 'To designate' is 'designare' — 'signare' means to seal or stamp

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'signatae erant'?

hints.perfect passive indicative: Perfect passive would be 'signatae sunt' — 'erant' (not 'sunt') makes this pluperfect
hints.imperfect passive indicative: Imperfect passive would be 'signabantur' — participle + 'erant' is pluperfect passive
hints.pluperfect active indicative: Pluperfect active would be 'signaverant' — participle + 'erant' is pluperfect passive

hints.significo, significare, significavi, sig: These are the parts of 'significare' (to signify) — a different compound
hints.designo, designare, designavi, designatu: These are the parts of 'designare' (to designate) — different prefix
hints.consigno, consignare, consignavi, consig: These are the parts of 'consignare' (to seal/record) — different prefix

steps.prompt: Why does the participle 'signatae' end in -ae?

hints.it is genitive singular: Genitive singular feminine would also be '-ae,' but here it matches nominative plural 'Litterae'
hints.it is dative singular: Dative singular would also be '-ae,' but the participle must agree with the subject 'Litterae'
hints.it is an adverb: Participles are not adverbs — 'signatae' is a perfect passive participle agreeing with 'Litterae'

steps.reveal: signo = with the seal (abl. of means)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'signum' — 'signo' with -o is ablative or dative
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'signum' — 'signo' is ablative of means here
hints.dative: While 'signo' could be dative, here it is ablative of means (sealed with the seal)

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'signi' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'signi' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'signi' ends in -i

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Lentulus' — 'Lentuli' with -i is genitive singular
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'Lentule' — 'Lentuli' is genitive of possession

steps.prompt: What type of ablative is 'signo Lentuli'?

hints.ablative absolute: Ablative absolute requires a participle — 'signo Lentuli' is just a noun phrase in the ablative
hints.ablative of manner: Ablative of manner typically uses 'cum' or a qualifying adjective — this expresses the instrument
hints.ablative of agent: Ablative of agent uses 'a/ab' + person — 'signo' is the thing (seal) used, not the agent

steps.prompt: Who was Lentulus in the Catilinarian conspiracy?
steps.target: P. Cornelius Lentulus Sura, a conspirator and sitting praetor
choices: P. Cornelius Lentulus Sura, a conspirator and sitting praetor
a military commander sent to fight Catiline

hints.a senator who opposed Catiline: Lentulus was one of the chief conspirators, not an opponent of Catiline
hints.a military commander sent to fight Catil: Lentulus was a conspirator who stayed in Rome — he was not sent to fight
hints.a witness for the prosecution: Lentulus was a defendant, not a witness — he was one of the key conspirators arrested

translation: The letters had been sealed with the seal of Lentulus
